evidence.support: The complete acquisition, transfer-learning, partitioning, testing, and evaluation workflow is described.

value: Acquire labeled melanoma and basal-cell-carcinoma images from four open repositories
Separate images into Fitzpatrick 1–3 and Fitzpatrick 4–5 cohorts
Create one Inception v3 model per skin-type cohort
Retain pretrained layers and retrain the final layer on dermatology images
Train each model on 150 images
Validate each model on 38 naïve images
Test each model on 30 held-out images
Calculate class-specific diagnostic metrics and ROC AUC
Compare the two skin-type-stratified model performances

evidence.support: The abstract states the comparison and principal conclusion.
The discussion and conclusion interpret the remaining performance difference.

value: An equal-image-count comparison of separate dermatology classifiers for Fitzpatrick 1–3 and Fitzpatrick 4–5
Evidence that equal image counts do not eliminate the darker-skin performance gap
A finding that more darker-skin disease images may be required to obtain comparable model performance

calibration_design.review_notes: No color, camera, illuminant, spectral, geometric, or cross-repository calibration procedure is reported.

camera_assumptions.review_notes: Camera spectral sensitivity, image signal processing, white balance, exposure, and device response are not reported or modeled.

capture_protocol.review_notes: The paper does not provide a standardized image capture protocol. It reuses images from multiple open dermatology repositories.

chromophore_representation.review_notes: Melanin, hemoglobin, and other chromophores are not measured or modeled.

evidence.support: The source specifies TensorFlow, Inception v3, pretraining, final-layer retraining, and probability outputs.

value.output: Class probabilities for melanoma and basal cell carcinoma
value.pretraining: All layers except the final layer pretrained on more than 1.2 million images
value.retraining: Final layer retrained on gathered dermatology images

evidence.support: The image repositories and disease classes are reported.
The skin-group-specific train, validation, and test sizes are reported.
The study identifies the classification metrics and explicitly defines the F1 score.

equations.review_notes: The source names standard diagnostic-classification metrics but does not print their complete formulas.
value: Sensitivity from true-positive and false-negative outcomes
Specificity from true-negative and false-positive outcomes
Positive predictive value
Negative predictive value
F1 score as the harmonic mean of recall and precision
Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve

evidence.support: The complete classification metric set is defined.

value: Sensitivity
Specificity
Positive predictive value
Negative predictive value
F1 score
ROC AUC

illumination_assumptions.review_notes: Illumination source, intensity, spectrum, uniformity, and control are not reported.

instrument_or_camera.review_notes: The source identifies public image repositories but does not establish the cameras, dermatoscopes, imaging devices, or acquisition hardware used to create the images.

evidence.support: The source identifies the image repositories, disease labels, and classification task.
The images are partitioned into light-skin and skin-of-color model cohorts.

value: Open-source dermatology images labeled as melanoma or basal cell carcinoma
Image grouping by Fitzpatrick types 1–3 or 4–5

evidence.support: The prediction outputs and evaluation quantities are specified.

value: Predicted melanoma probability
Predicted basal-cell-carcinoma probability
Ground-truth diagnostic class
Sensitivity
Specificity
Positive predictive value
Negative predictive value
F1 score
Area under the receiver operating characteristic

physical_model.review_notes: The paper does not define a light–tissue, reflectance, absorption, or image-formation model.

evidence.support: The abstract states lower model performance for Fitzpatrick types 4 and 5.
The introduction identifies darker-skin performance degradation and asks whether it persists under equal image counts.

problem_addressed.value: Dermatology image-recognition systems perform less effectively on images from darker Fitzpatrick skin classifications, potentially because of both data scarcity and lower visual contrast between lesions and surrounding skin.

evidence.support: The paper explicitly identifies image scarcity, small sample size, darker-skin presentation variability, and the need for more images.

reported_limitations.review_notes: The acquisition, patient-level, and representation boundaries follow from information absent from the reported design and from its explicit use of grouped Fitzpatrick categories.
value: The available number of Fitzpatrick 4–5 images was limited.
Both models were constrained to the same small training-set size because of darker-skin image scarcity.
A larger sample was needed to test whether the result would recur consistently.
The source does not establish acquisition standardization across its four repositories.
The source does not report patient-level independence, demographic composition, or external validation.
Fitzpatrick grouping is coarse and is not a continuous measurement of skin color.

evidence.support: The class-specific metrics, AUC values, average AUC, and comparative finding are reported.

value.overall_finding: The Fitzpatrick 1–3 models outperformed the Fitzpatrick 4–5 models across the reported classification measures.

evidence.support: The source reports image-level cohorts, disease labels, and Fitzpatrick groups.

sample_or_subject_population.review_notes: The evidence unit is the image. The source does not establish whether multiple images came from the same patient.

scattering_representation.review_notes: Optical scattering is not measured or modeled.

evidence.support: The abstract defines the two-class task and equal-image-count comparison.
The study objective explicitly compares performance under equal image counts.

scientific_objective.value: Determine whether equal-sized deep-learning models trained, validated, and tested separately on Fitzpatrick types 1–3 and types 4–5 differ in their ability to classify melanoma versus basal cell carcinoma.

evidence.support: The abstract defines Fitzpatrick groups 1–3 and 4–5.
The model cohorts and performance table are stratified by the two Fitzpatrick groups.

value.role: Dataset-stratification variable for separate model training, validation, testing, and performance comparison

evidence.support: The equal-sized Fitzpatrick-stratified design and performance disparity are explicit.
The darker-skin data and visual-presentation limitations are explicit.

value.boundary: The paper evaluates downstream disease classification using coarse Fitzpatrick groups. It does not measure true skin color, recover reflectance, normalize illumination, calibrate camera response, or establish a device-independent color representation.
directly_relevant_findings: Model performance differs across grouped Fitzpatrick skin classifications even under equal image counts
Darker-skin dermatology images are less available
Darker-skin lesion presentation may have lower contrast against surrounding skin
Validation must be stratified by skin representation rather than reported only as an aggregate

evidence.support: Training, naïve validation, held-out testing, equal cohort sizes, and balanced disease classes are specified.

value.comparison: Separate but equal-sized models for Fitzpatrick 1–3 and Fitzpatrick 4–5

title: Performance of Artificial Intelligence Imaging Models in Detecting Dermatological Manifestations in Higher Fitzpatrick Skin Color Classifications
